coredata.dc:description: Degradable fluorescent sensors that can avoid the secondary contamination are highly ideal for the detection of heavy metal ions, aligning with the sustainable development principle, thus becoming the subject of considerable interest. In this work, a carbon dot-based self-degradable pre‑formed particle gel (CDs@DPPG) was successfully synthesized by one-step free radical polymerization with green fluorescence-emitted carbon dots as fluorescence probes, acrylamide (AM) and 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propanesulfonic acid (AMPS) as monomers and 1,6-hexanediol diacrylate (HDDA) as crosslinkers. Results show that CDs@DPPGs exhibit a significant quenching response, good selectivity, and fast response to Fe3+. Specifically, this gel can spontaneously degrade to a liquid with the viscosity of ca. 2.7 mPa s at a certain temperature, resulting from the presence of the thermodegradable HDDA, which is beneficial for the subsequent processing after the test. Here, the porous network structure of the CDs@DPPG provides not only a large number of active sites but also the enhanced ion transport channels, thereby reducing fluorescence response time and improving detection sensitivity. Our findings suggest that the strategy proposed in this work, i.e., combining the degradable gel with the CD-based fluorescence probe, holds a promising potential in creating the green and sustainable fluorescent sensors.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: FT-IR spectra (a) and UV-Vis spectrum (b) of the CDs, DPPG and CDs@DPPG. Inset: UV-Vis spectrum of DPPG, and the fluorescence image of the CDs@DPPG (right) and DPPG (left) under UV light.
Fluorescence emission spectra of the CDs@DPPG under different excitation wavelengths. Inset: the variation of the maximum emission peak intensity with the excitation wavelength.

Fluorescence properties of CDs@DPPG as a function of diverse variables in solutions: (a) temperature, (b) pH, (c) NaCl salinity and (d) CaCl2 salinity. Inset: the separate variation of maximum fluorescence intensity with the individual variable.
The changes in fluorescence intensity during heating-cooling cycles between 25 and 85 °C.

(a) Fluorescence intensity of the CDs@DPPG under different metal ions with metal concentration of 0.01 mol L−1, (b) I/I 0 value for each metal ion, (c) Fluorescence intensity of CDs@DPPG in the presence of coexisting metal ions with the concentration of 0.01 mol L−1, (d) I/I 0 value in the presence of coexisting metal ions, (e) Fluorescence intensity of the CDs@DPPG at different Fe3+ ion concentration, and (f) the I/I 0 value of CDs@DPPG as a function of immersion time in Fe3+ solution.
(a) Photographs of spontaneous degradation process of CDs@DPPGs at 80 °C. (b) Swelling ability of CDs@DPPG particles. (c) Viscosity of CDs@DPPGs after complete degradation.

Swelling and degradation performances of CDs@DPPGs as a function of diverse variables in the synthesis process: (a) total amounts of monomers (AM and AMPS), (b) ratios of AM/AMPS, dosage of (c) crosslinker (HDDA) and (d) initiator (KPS).
The formulations of CDs@DPPG with different compositions and the values of swelling ratio (SR) and degradation time (DT).

head.ce:title: A novel carbon dot-based degradable pre‑formed particle gel for sensitive detection of iron ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering and School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai 200237, P. R. China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering and School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Chemical Engineering and School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology, Shanghai, 200237, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Degradable fluorescent sensors that can avoid the secondary contamination are highly ideal for the detection of heavy metal ions, aligning with the sustainable development principle, thus becoming the subject of considerable interest. In this work, a carbon dot-based self-degradable pre‑formed particle gel (CDs@DPPG) was successfully synthesized by one-step free radical polymerization with green fluorescence-emitted carbon dots as fluorescence probes, acrylamide (AM) and 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propanesulfonic acid (AMPS) as monomers and 1,6-hexanediol diacrylate (HDDA) as crosslinkers. Results show that CDs@DPPGs exhibit a significant quenching response, good selectivity, and fast response to Fe3+. Specifically, this gel can spontaneously degrade to a liquid with the viscosity of ca. 2.7 mPa s at a certain temperature, resulting from the presence of the thermodegradable HDDA, which is beneficial for the subsequent processing after the test. Here, the porous network structure of the CDs@DPPG provides not only a large number of active sites but also the enhanced ion transport channels, thereby reducing fluorescence response time and improving detection sensitivity. Our findings suggest that the strategy proposed in this work, i.e., combining the degradable gel with the CD-based fluorescence probe, holds a promising potential in creating the green and sustainable fluorescent sensors.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: With the development of industrialization, the pollution of heavy metal ions that has adverse effects on environmental safety and human health has continued to be of serious concern all over the world [1,2]. The overloading of the heavy metal ions (e.g., Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+) is highly toxic to biological organisms, which can cause irreversible and dangerous oxidative stress and serious damage to the central nervous system, hence leading to fatal renal and neural problems [3–5]. Accordingly, the rapid and high-efficiency detection of such heavy metal ions is of great significance for the effective control of their pollution, from both environmental safety and human health standpoints. Up to now, several well-established methods have been reported for the detection of heavy metal ions, such as atomic absorption [6,7], inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry [8,9], chemiluminescence method [10] and electrochemical analysis [11–13]. Despite numerous attempts to achieve excellent detection limits and accurate quantifications, these methods do not meet the requirements for simple and rapid detection of the metals in practical applications due to the complexity of the sample preparation, the high cost of instrument and the tedious analytical method [14–16]. Therefore, an efficient, simple and sensitive method for detecting and quantifying the heavy metal ions in aqueous media remains to be established in a diversity of practical applications.

Carbon dots (CDs), also known as carbon nanoparticles, are the latest addition to the carbon-based fluorescent nanomaterial family and considered one of the most ideal fluorescent materials due to their attractive properties such as facile synthesis, biocompatibility, physicochemical stability and unique optical characteristic [17]. To date, CDs have received extensive attention and found widespread applications in various field, comprising biosensors [18,19], medical diagnostics [20,21], fluorescent sensors [22,23], photocatalysis [24] and full-color displays [25]. Especially, CDs have been recently proved to be a promising fluorescent sensor to detect heavy metal ions due to the unique size and functional structure [26–29], based on the fluorescence quenching effect caused by interactions between metal ions and fluorophore functional groups. However, the main problem that carbon dot-based sensors are facing is that the bare CDs tend to aggregate under neutral conditions, leading to a dramatic decrease in fluorescence intensity [30]. To stabilize CDs and improve their performance, the immobilization of CDs within solid matrix has been pursued extensively in recent years. In general, the quasi-solid platform helps to maintain the properties of CDs in solution, prevent the fluorescence quenching caused by self-aggregation of CDs through either electrostatic interactions, noncovalent bonds, covalent bonds or hydrogen bonds [31,32], and immobilize and disperse CDs in matrices uniformly [33]. Among the variety of solid matrices, the hydrogel is preferred over other frameworks for immobilizing CDs because of its transparency, semi-moisturizing properties, simple processing, and highly tunable three-dimensional porous structure. Moreover, the high specific surface area enables the hydrogel to accommodate a wide range of micro- and nano-particles, which can effectively prevent the aggregation of carbon dots and greatly improve their fluorescence stability. To date, several studies on loading CDs into hydrogel networks have been reported [34–36]. A fluorescent smart CD/hydrogel nanocomposite was prepared via embedding polyethyleneimine surface-modified CDs into microcrystalline cellulose hydrogels [34], which exhibited intense blue emission at 445 nm with high stability and good response to Fe3+. A CD-based agarose hydrogel hybrid film was used for optical detection and separation of diverse heavy metal ions, e.g., Cr6+, Cu2+, Fe3+, Pb2+ and Mn2+ [35]. A fluorescent hydrogel, obtained from incorporating CDs modified with glycidyl methacrylate into poly(N-isopropylacrylamide) hydrogel matrix, exhibited the favorable quenching response and high adsorption capacity to metallic iron ions, resulted from the chemical linkage between CD and matrix [36]. Despite these pioneering sensors having highly selective capabilities for heavy metal ion, the quasi-solid and non-degradable matrix may pose a risk of secondary contamination if managed improperly. In order to address this issue as well as fulfill a task-specific requirement, a strategy of combining the degradable hydrogel with fluorescence-emitted CD was proposed in this work, with a view to achieving a rapid and sensitive detection of heavy metal ions first and then a self-degradation of matrix upon command.

In this study, the primary purpose is to prepare a fluorescent gel with a thermal degradation attribute for the detection of Fe(III) ions, as shown in Scheme 1. The CDs, with the green fluorescence-emitted properties, were first prepared using a hydrothermal method with ascorbic acid. The carbon dot-based self-degradable pre‑formed particle gel (CDs@DPPG) was then successfully obtained via one-step free radical polymerization reaction with the green fluorescence-emitted carbon dot (CD) as fluorescence probe, acrylamide (AM) and 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propanesulfonic acid (AMPS) as the monomers, 1,6-hexanediol diacrylate (HDDA) as the crosslinking agent and potassium persulfate (KPS) as the initiator. The inherent porous network structure of the CDs@DPPG plays a pivotal role in defining the interaction between the Fe3+ ions and CDs and significantly improves the ion transport capacity and the availability of active sites, thereby dramatically enhancing the detection sensitivity and response speed. Especially, the CDs@DPPG can spontaneously degrade to a liquid with a low viscosity at a certain temperature, which paves a new way for the subsequent reprocessing without any other handling, aligning seamlessly with the prerequisites of a sustainable development concept. Thus, the strategy proposed in this work, i.e., combining degradable gels and CD-based fluorescent probes, holds great promise for creating green and sustainable fluorescent sensors, providing new ideas for novel fluorescent detection platforms.

All of the chemicals and reagents, including ascorbic acid (AA, 99.7 %), citric acid (CA, 99.5 %), polyethylenimine (PEI, 99 %, Mw = 1800), acrylamide (AM, 99.0 %), 2-acrylamido-2-methyl-1-propanesulfonic acid (AMPS, 98.0 %), 1,6-hexanediol diacrylate (HDDA, 90.0 %), potassium persulfate (KPS, 99.9 %), sodium chloride (NaCl, 99.9 %), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 99.9 %), sodium hydroxide (NaOH, 99.9 %), hydrochloric acid (HCl, 99.9 %), chromium trichloride (CrCl3, 99.0 %), manganese chloride (MnCl2, 99.0 %), ferric chloride (FeCl3, 99.0 %), ferrous chlorid（FeCl2, 99.0 %), cobaltous chloride (CoCl2, 99.7 %), nickel chloride (NiCl2, 99.0 %), cupric chloride (CuCl2, 98.0 %) and zinc chloride (ZnCl2, 98.0 %), were purchased from Shanghai Macklin Biological Co., Ltd, China and used as received. House-deionized water was purified using a LD-WFUP ultrapure water filtering system comprised of one carbon stage and two ion-exchange stages.

All CDs used in the experiments were synthesized using AA, PEI and CA as raw materials according to the procedure reported previously [37]. In brief, 0.15 g of ascorbic acid (AA), 0.18 g of polyethylenimine (PEI) and 0.8 g of citric acid (CA) were dissolved in 10 mL of ultrapure water, the mixture was then stirred at room temperature for 5 min. Subsequently, this solution was transferred to an autoclave for further reaction at 150 °C for 1 h. After the reaction was completed, it was naturally cooled to ambient temperature, affording an aqueous solution of CDs.

In a typical procedure, the as-synthesized CDs were ultrasonically dispersed in ultrapure water. An appropriate amount of AM and AMPS solid powder was added into the aqueous CD dispersion and stirred on a magnetic stirrer at a certain speed, followed by adjusting the pH value to neutral. Then, a certain amount of HDDA and KPS was weighed and added to the above stirring dispersion. The reaction was allowed to proceed at 45 °C for 4 h in nitrogen atmosphere. After gelation, the gel was removed and rinsed with ultrapure water for several times, then dried by freeze dryer, and finally pulverized into particles.

The structure of the CDs@DPPG was investigated on Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Nicolet IS10, ThermoFisher, USA). Prior to tests, CDs@DPPG particles were dried and ground with KBr powder, the mixture was then compressed into a pellet for FT-IR analysis. The optical properties of the CDs@DPPG were acquired using UV-Visible spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Japan) and fluorescence spectrophotometer (LS55, PerkinElmer Company, Waltham, MA, USA). The rheology properties of the CDs@DPPG were measured on a rheometer (MCR 302e, Anton Paar) after complete degradation. Typically, the degraded solution was placed on a sample stage and gradually heated to a preset temperature to determine the change in viscosity as a function of shear rate.

To demonstrate that the gel has a fluorescence quenching response towards Fe3+, the smart CDs@DPPG was immersed in the target solutions containing different concentrations of Fe3+ ion as well as different interfering ions at the same concentration. The fluorescence intensity of CDs@DPPG was then tested using a fluorescence spectrophotometer at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The selectivity of the smart gel toward Fe3+ ion was evaluated by adding other common metal ions, including Na+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+. To ensure the accuracy of the experiments, all experiments were performed individually and triplicate samples were taken at least in each case.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of swelling and degradation properties of CDs@DPPG
ce:section.ce:para:
The swelling behavior of the CDs@DPPG was carried out in ultrapure water according to the protocol reported previously [38]. Briefly, a certain amount of dried CDs@DPPG particles were weighed and placed in home-made tea bags, and then immersed into aqueous solution to attain equilibrium state at room temperature after a certain period of time. In this work, the variation in the weight of the CDs@DPPG particle with the swelling time was recorded. The swelling ratio (SR) at any time interval t is defined as follows:
S R
( % )
=
W s − W o
W o
× 100 %
where W o and W s are the original and swollen weight of CDs@DPPG particles, respectively.

After swelling process reached equilibrium, the tea bags were withdrawn from water and removed excessive fluid on surfaces with filter paper. Then, the swollen CDs@DPPG was placed in a thermostat at a preset temperature to determine the degradation properties of the CDs@DPPG and the entire degradation time, defined as the time when the sample varies from a solid gel to a flowable liquid without visible particles [39].

The FT-IR spectra of the CDs, DPPG and CDs@DPPG were firstly examined to validate the formation of the weak interactions between CDs and the DPPG, as shown in Fig. 1a. In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs, a broad peak centered at 3481 cm–1 is attributed to the cooperative effect of the stretching vibrations of O−H and N−H. Two weak but visible peaks at 2957 and 2839 cm–1 are ascribed to the stretching vibration of C−H, while another two remarkably characteristic peaks at 1709 and 1559 cm–1 are assigned to the stretching vibrations of CO and CC, respectively [37]. In the FT-IR spectrum of DPPG, the broad peak at 3342 cm–1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of N − H, whereas the characteristic peak at 1653 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CO in the amide group, which is the typical peak of polyacrylamide. In particular, two notable peaks at 1180 and 1040 cm−1 are associated with asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of the sulfonate anions, −SO3 −, respectively [40], which lends support for the successful incorporation of AMPS in DPPG. In the FT-IR spectrum of CDs@DPPG, two typical features are observed: i) the introduction of CDs leads to an appreciable red-shift from 3481 to 3340 cm–1 for the stretching vibrations of O−H and N−H of CDs and from 3342 to 3340 for the stretching vibration of N−H of DPPG, respectively, meaning that the hydrogen bonds are formed between CDs and DPPGs; ii) after incorporating the CDs into the DDPG matrix, the position of the other characteristic peaks is almost unchanged, which provides another evidence for that CDs are embedded into the DPPG matrix mainly through weak interactions rather than chemical bonds [41].

In this work, UV-Vis spectra of CDs, DPPG and CDs@DPPG were conducted to determine their absorbance in the wavelength range from 250 to 500 nm. As shown in Fig. 1b, CDs and CDs@DPPG both exhibit a characteristic peak at the maximum absorption wavelength (λ max) of λ max = 350 nm, which may be caused by the n-π* transition of the structure of the CO groups [42], while DPPG does not show any characteristic absorption, so that its curve is not included, but given in the inset. Here, CDs@DPPG and CD differ significantly in the shape and intensity of the absorption peaks, though they possess the same position of λ max, which is a likely result of the change in the micro-environment of CDs after being embedded into DPPGs. More importantly, DPPG itself does not exhibit any perceptible fluorescence signal under UV light, while CDs@DPPG displays a profound fluorescence attribute, i.e., the bright green luminescence, as shown in the inset. This indicates that CDs have been embedded into DPPG, that is, CDs@DPPG has been successfully synthesized.

CDs@DPPG exhibits a typical excitation-dependent photoluminescence (PL) behavior, as given in Fig. 2. Here, the maximum emission peak of the CDs@DPPG redshifts, when the excitation wavelength (λ ex) is varied from 330 to 400 nm. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity increases first and then decreases with the increased excitation wavelength, and reaches the maximum at λ ex = ∼ 350 nm, as shown in the inset (Fig. 2). These results are consistent with the photoluminescence properties of most of CDs reported previously [43–45].

The effects of diverse variables, including temperature, pH, NaCl/CaCl2 concentration, on the fluorescence intensity were investigated at λ ex = 350 nm, in consideration of the maximum PL intensity acquired at this λ ex. Obviously, CDs@DPPG exhibits an explicit temperature-dependent PL behavior as the temperature increases from 25 to 85 °C ( Fig. 3a), that is, the fluorescence intensity gradually decreases with the increased temperature, as shown in the inset. This can be attributed to the increased temperature that may play two roles in determining PL behaviors: i) the formation of the more non-radiation channels activated thermally, by which an increasing number of the excited electrons can return to their ground state, and ii) the occurrence of the CD aggregation, which can significantly quench the fluorescence, thus yielding temperature-dependent luminescence [46,47]. In Fig. 3b is shown the PL intensity of CDs@DPPGs as a function of the pH value of solution at 25 °C. Interestingly, CDs@DPPGs also possess a pH-sensitive PL feature, namely that they give a monotonic reduction in the fluorescence intensity with the raised pH, as shown in the inset. This pH-dependent fluorescence arises from the protonation and deprotonation of carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs [48]. In acidic solutions, a high level of carboxylic acid group is generally left on the surface of the CDs, which implies a large number of the fluorophores are remained, thus affording intensive PL emissions. In contrast, the deprotonation of carboxyl groups commonly takes place in alkaline solutions, which reduces the amount of fluorophore species and consequently weakens the PL intensity. In addition, another reason why the pronounced fluorescence quenching occurs at high pH value is the pH-induced aggregation of CDs due to intermolecular noncovalent interactions [49]. More strikingly, the salinity of monovalent (NaCl) and divalent (CaCl2) salts almost has a negligible effect on the fluorescence intensity of CDs@DPPGs, as shown in Fig. 3c and d, respectively. In this work, the increase in the salinity of NaCl and CaCl2 (from 10,000 to 30,000 mg/L) results in the reduction in the PL intensity of ca. 10 % for NaCl and 7.1 % for CaCl2, respectively. This implies that CDs@DPPGs have a promising potential for achieving the specific detection for a given cation, if the target cation can effectively quench their fluorescence, because the other monovalent or divalent salts, e.g., alkali and alkaline earth metal ions used here, do not interfere this specific response [50,51].

In this work, CDs@DPPGs present a fascinatingly temperature-responsive reversible switching, as shown in Fig. 4, in spite of decreasing the PL peak intensity induced by the enhanced temperature. Here, CDs@DPPGs can reversibly switch between “on” and “off” states: the fluorescence of CDs@DPPGs shows the “off” state at high temperature (85 °C), while it is “on” at low temperature (25 °C). Moreover, CDs@DPPGs still give a good repeatability after 5 heating/cooling cycles, where the fluorescence intensity remains unaltered. This indicates that CDs@DPPGs have a great temperature-resistant behavior, which can be used over a wide temperature range.

In principle, some metal ions can effectively quench the fluorescence of CDs by means of electrostatic attraction and/or chelation with surface functional groups, e.g., carboxyl and amino groups, thus creating the fluorescence sensor with specific response to them, if without the interference of other ions. In this work, the as-prepared CDs@DPPGs were used for the detection of iron (Fe3+) ions. To demonstrate the selectivity of CDs@DPPGs in detecting iron ions, the effects of several common metal cations (Na+, Ca2+, Cr3+, Mn2+, Fe3+, Fe2+, Co2+, Ni2+, Cu2+ and Zn2+) on the fluorescence emission of the CDs@DPPG were explored; meanwhile, to eliminate the interference of anions in the experiments, chloride salts were used. Fig. 5a shows the fluorescence response of CDs@DPPGs to all metal ions at a certain concentration of 0.01 mol L−1 as well as in ultrapure water. For clarity, the ratio of I/I 0 for different ions is also given as a histogram (Fig. 5b), where I 0 and I represent the fluorescence intensity without and with the addition of metal ions, respectively. Obviously, the addition of Fe3+ leads to a remarkable fluorescence quenching of the CDs@DPPG, having I/I 0 = 0.0121, while other metal ions have a slight or close to negligible effect on the fluorescent intensity, relying on the ion species selected, of which I/I 0 varies from 0.746 to 0.969 (more details see Table S1). This specific fluorescence quenching action may be attributed to the formation of CDs-Fe3+ complexes, in which the electronegative O and N groups (such as carboxyl, amino groups, etc.) on the surface of CDs can associate with Fe3+ to form coordination complexes. After the formation of CDs-Fe3+ complexes, the electrons on the excited states of CDs are easily transferred to the half-filled 3d orbitals of Fe3+ ions, concurrently accompanied with energy transfer, to form nonradiative recombination, thus resulting in fluorescence quenching [52,53]. Additionally, the high selectivity of CDs for Fe3+ may also be attributed to the higher thermodynamic affinity towards Fe3+ ion than other metal ions as well as the faster chelation with CDs [54]. Taken together, these results indicate that the CDs@DPPG is highly selective for Fe3+ and can be used as a fluorescent probe with a specific response to detect Fe3+ in various real-world applications.

In order to judge whether other metal ions have an interfering effect on the Fe3+ detection of CDs@DPPGs, the quenching effect of Fe3+ in the presence of coexisting metal ions was also studied, as shown in Fig. 5c. Similarly, in Fig. 5d is shown the histogram of I/I 0 values for all interfering ions. The I/I 0 values vary from 0.0120 to 0.0129 (more details see Table S2), after separately adding interfering ions, which are close to that (0.0123) in the case of the addition of Fe3+ only. This further suggests that CDs@DPPGs possess the high selectivity for Fe3+. Furthermore, the fluorescence response of CDs@DPPGs to Fe3+ at different concentrations was also investigated with a view to determining the specific fluorescence sensing behavior, as given in Fig. 5e. Evidently, CDs@DPPGs exhibit a marked reduction in the fluorescence intensity with the increased Fe3+ concentrations, that is, the higher the Fe3+ concentration, the more pronounced the fluorescence quenching effect. In addition, the response time of fluorescence quenching, as another essential parameter for evaluating the fluorescence detection performance of fluorescent probes, was further investigated in this work, as shown in Fig. 5f. As is apparent, the fluorescence peak intensity excited at λ ex = 350 nm remains almost unchanged only 2 min later after being immersed in the Fe3+ solution, where the I/I 0 value is ca. 0.0123, very close to the original value of 0.0121, which demonstrates that the quenching effect of Fe3+ with respect to CDs@DPPGs is extremely rapid and stable, which can reach the equilibrium in ca. 2 min (here, it is too rapid to detect the varying process of fluorescence within 2 min). This rapid response may be attributed to CDs@DPPGs that have the excellent interconnected porous network, favoring the transport of Fe3+, similar to that reported in other hydrogels [55,56].

At present, although hydrogel-based fluorescent sensors used for the selective detection of Fe3+ ions have been extensively investigated [57–59], they are non-degradable, posing a risk of secondary contamination if treated improperly. In CDs@DPPG composites obtained in this work, DPPGs can spontaneously degrade to a low-viscosity liquid at a certain temperature, which can effectively address the above issue and thus promise substantial environmental and economic benefits. Here, the spontaneous degradation performance of CDs@DPPGs was studied using bottle test ( Fig. 6a) and viscosity measurement (Fig. 6c), while their swelling ability was examined using the method mentioned in experimental section (Fig. 6b). CDs@DPPGs can directly change from a solid gel to a flowable liquid at 80 °C over time, intuitively demonstrating the degradable nature. Fig. 6b shows the SR of thoroughly dried CDs@DPPGs against the immersing time. The SR increases rapidly at the initial stage, then levels off and finally attains the saturated state. After 300 min, the SR exceeds 4000 %. This result indicates that CDs@DPPGs have a high affinity for water, which is a likely consequence of the both monomers, i.e., AM and AMPS, which have an excellent hydrophilicity, thereby favoring the interaction with water and its invasion into the interchain void when CDs@DPPGs encounter water. In addition, in order to evaluate the degradation degree of DPPGs, the viscosity of CDs@DPPGs after complete degradation was also investigated. At the shear rate exceeding 15 s−1, the viscosity of the degraded liquid almost maintains at ∼2.7 mPa s, which is of the same order of magnitude as pure water. This suggests that no extra treatment is required after CDs@DPPGs spontaneously degrade in practical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of different conditions on self-degradation and swelling performance
ce:section.ce:para: In order to meet different filed conditions of CDs@DPPG applications, the effects of monomer dosage, relative ratio of AM/AMPS, crosslinker content and amount added of initiator on the swelling ability and spontaneous degradation performance of CDs@DPPGs were systematically investigated in this work. In brief, CDs@DPPGs were first immersed in the water to measure the swelling capacity, and then placed in an oven thermostatted to test the degradation properties. More details about the formulations of how to synthesize CDs@DPPGs and the values of swelling ratio (SR) and degradation time (DT) are compiled in Table 1, the corresponding experimental results are shown in Fig. 7.

Fig. 7a shows the effect of the monomer concentration on swelling ability and self-degradation time of the CDs@DPPGs, where entries from N1 to N5 in Table 1 correspond to the specific compositions of CDs@DPPGs. Obviously, increasing the monomer dosage, while holding the AM/AMPS ratio and the content of crosslinker and initiator unvaried, may lead to a progressive reduction in the swelling capacity of CDs@DPPGs. The CDs@DPPG related to N1 exhibits the highest swelling capacity of SR = ∼4440 % after the swelling reaches equilibrium, while it associated with N5 has the lowest SR (∼2220 %). In contrast to the swelling capacity, the complete degradation time presents an opposite trend, namely that CDs@DPPGs display a successive growth in the complete degradation time, from about 100 h (N1, 1 g) to 290 h (N5, 5 g), with the total concentration of monomers. The variation of the swelling behavior of CDs@DPPGs with the monomeric dosage can be attributed to the increased total amount of monomers that bring in the elevated crosslinking density in DPPGs, thus narrowing the pore size and reducing the number of free voids available for water accommodation. This may lead to a decrease in the value of osmotic force driving water molecules into the DPPG network, so that the swelling capacity of CDs@DPPGs reduces [60]. On the contrary, the improved crosslinking density can generally form a relatively stable three-dimensional network of hydrogels and promote the mechanical strength to some extent, resulting in an extended degradation time of CDs@DPPGs.

In this work, the impact of only varying the ratio of AM/AMPS, while maintaining other variables (i.e., total amount of monomers, dosage of crosslinker (HDDA) and initiator (KPS)) constant, on the swelling and self-degradability of CDs@DPPGs was also studied, as given in Fig. 7b. The entries from N6 to N10 in Table 1 give the detailed compositions of synthesizing CDs@DPPGs with different ratio of AM/AMPS. The enhanced ratio of AM/AMPS significantly reduces swelling ability of CDs@DPPGs. The CDs@DPPG associated with N6 exhibits the highest swelling capacity of SR = 4880 %, which approximately increases by a factor of 49, after the swelling reaches equilibrium. In comparison, the CDs@DPPG corresponding to N10 only shows a ca. 31-fold enhancement in the swelling capacity, having SR = 3120 %. Similarly, five CDs@DPPGs from N6 to N10 also exhibit an opposite varying trend in the complete degradation time, with respect to the swelling performance. Here, the complete degradation time of CDs@DPPGs continuously grows from ca. 60 (N6) to 250 (N10) h, as the AM/AMPS ratio varies from 3:7 to 7:3. The reduced swelling behavior of CDs@DPPGs can be ascribed to the difference in hydrophilicity between AM and AMPS groups, where AMPS has a higher affinity for water relative to AM, which can generate a high swelling pressure, thus absorbing large amounts of water [61]. In addition, a clear inverse relationship exists between the complete self-degradation time and the final swelling ratio. Although the underlying reason is still unclear at present, a possible explanation is that AMPS has a relatively high molecular weight, so that increasing the AMPS proportion would lead to the reduced crosslinking density in networks, thus weakening the mechanical strength.

In Fig. 7c is shown the effect of crosslinker concentration on both swelling performances and self-degradation abilities of CDs@DPPGs, which correspond to five samples from N11 to N15 in Table 1. Also, increasing the crosslinker dosage from N11 (200 mg) to N15 (400 mg), while holding the AM/AMPS ratio and the amount of monomer and initiator unvaried, leads to a progressive reduction in the swelling capacity of CDs@DPPGs from SR = 4100 % (N11) to SR = 2130 %. This is a likely result of the increased dosage of crosslinker that improves the crosslinking density of DPPGs, thus restricting the motion of polymer segments and lowering the water the absorption capacity of CDs@DPPGs [62]; and that enhances the rigidity of DPPGs, which can reduce the osmotic pressure between DPPGs and external solvent, detrimental to water molecules entering the inside of CDs@DPPGs, thus leading to a lower swelling capacity. Nevertheless, the complete degradation time of CDs@DPPGs gradually increases from about 120 (N11) to 370 h (N15) with the added amount of crosslinker, which can be also attributed to the augmented crosslinking density and hence the mechanical properties.

The final point that deserves comment is the effect of the initiator concentration on the swelling capacity and complete degradation time of CDs@DPPGs. In this regard, the corresponding compositions of CDs@DPPGs are compiled in Table 1, i.e., from N16 to N20. Obviously, the swelling capacity increases gradually from SR = 3690 % (N16) to SR = 4620 % (N20), whereas the complete degradation time decreases continuously from approximately 170 h (N16) to 80 h (N20), as the initiator concentration increases, while remaining the AM/AMPS ratio and the concentration of monomer and cross‑linker constant. These results may be caused by the increased quantity of free radicals in CDs@DPPGs, resulting from the grown content of initiator, which can offer a higher probability of free radical collision and rate of polymerization [63], but afford the lower polymerization degree and the lower microscopic crosslinking density as well, thus causing the higher swelling ability and the shorter degradation time.

The results of our study demonstrate that the fluorescent gel with a thermal degradation can be used for the specific detection of Fe3+. This method proposed in our work effectively solves the problem of secondary contamination caused by improper treatment, providing a new idea for the research of the novel fluorescent detection platforms. Despite the contributions of our work, the following two points should be pointed out: (i) the species of ions detected in our work are only restricted to Fe3+ and its detection sensitivity is relatively limited, compared with the existed studies, so that the specific responsiveness to other metal ions and their high sensitivity are required; (ii) the proportion of monomers, initiators and crosslinkers is needed to be further defined in the synthesis of degradable fluorescent gels based on the existing literature to expand application range.

In short, CDs@DPPGs with fluorescent and degradable behaviors were prepared using a simple way and their properties were investigated. CDs@DPPGs exhibit an obvious fluorescence quenching effect upon responding to Fe3+, which features high selectivity and rapid responsiveness. Especially, CDs@DPPGs can spontaneously and thoroughly degrade to a liquid with the viscosity of ca. 2.7 mPa s at a certain temperature, which offers great opportunities to meet the field applications without extra treating in the subsequent processes. Our findings suggest that CDs@DPPGs not only constitute a sustainable sensing platform, but also highlight a potential to address the challenges posed by the heavy metal ion detection. In a broader context, the strategy of combining fluorescent CDs with degradable hydrogels is conducive to extending the practical application scopes of both materials, i.e., specific sensing, stimuli-responsiveness, etc.

Lu Li: Software, Methodology. Fang Wang: Validation, Formal analysis. Wenhui Gong: Software. Meng Li: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation. Yuhuan Nie: Software, Methodology. Qibin Chen: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Data curation.
